title: Climate Change and Infectious Disease
authors: Aaron S. Bernstein, Jonathan A. Patz

items.subtopic: Climate Change and Infectious Disease | OVERVIEW
items.title: Impact of Climate Change on Infectious Diseases
items.content: The alteration of climate due to human activities has already changed the epidemiology of infectious diseases. The ongoing accumulation of greenhouse gases is expected to further influence the incidence and severity of infections, potentially leading to both the emergence of new diseases and the disappearance of existing ones in certain areas.
keyPoints: Greenhouse gas emissions have altered climate since the late nineteenth century.
Climate change has already impacted infectious disease epidemiology.
Future changes may increase or decrease suitability for certain diseases.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · Climate Change and Infectious Disease | OVERVIEW

items.subtopic: Climate Change and Infectious Disease | OVERVIEW
items.title: Unprecedented Rate of Current Climate Change
items.content: The current climate change is distinct due to its rapid pace, primarily driven by human activities. Unlike past natural cycles, the present warming rate is unprecedented in the last 50 million years, with significant temperature increases potentially occurring within a century.
keyPoints: Current climate change is faster than any period in the last 50 million years.
The end of the last ice age saw a 5°C increase over approximately 5000 years.
A similar temperature rise may occur within the next 150 years without reducing greenhouse gas emissions.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · Climate Change and Infectious Disease | OVERVIEW

items.title: Impact of Greenhouse Gas Concentrations on Climate Change and Infectious Diseases
items.content: Increased concentrations of greenhouse gases, particularly carbon dioxide and methane, have significantly contributed to global warming. This warming is unevenly distributed across the globe, with regions like the Arctic experiencing faster temperature increases. These changes in climate can alter patterns of infectious diseases, especially vector-borne illnesses.
keyPoints: Carbon dioxide and methane concentrations have increased considerably since the nineteenth century, with methane more than doubling.
The average surface temperature of Earth has risen by 1.09°C from 1880 to 2020.
The Arctic region is warming twice as fast overall compared to other regions.
Nighttime minimum temperatures are rising faster than daytime high temperatures.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · GREENHOUSE GASES | TEMPERATURE

items.title: Consequences of Extreme Heat Waves on Human Health and Agriculture
items.content: Climate change is leading to more frequent, longer, and severe heat waves. These events have direct impacts on human health, causing increased morbidity and mortality, and also affect agriculture by damaging crops. The 2010 Russian heat wave serves as a notable example of these consequences.
keyPoints: The hottest 5-year period recorded was from 2016 to 2020.
Heat waves contribute directly to human morbidity and mortality.
In 2010, the Russian heat wave led to hundreds of forest fires, killing an estimated 56,000 people and burning 300,000 acres of crops.
Approximately 25% of Russia's wheat fields were destroyed during this heat wave.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · GREENHOUSE GASES | TEMPERATURE

items.title: Impact of Climate Change on Precipitation Patterns and Associated Health Risks
items.content: Climate change has altered the Earth's water cycle, leading to increased heavy precipitation events in Europe and North America. This is due to a warmer atmosphere holding more water vapor, which promotes heavier rainfall in traditionally wet areas while increasing drought risk in arid regions.
keyPoints: Substantial increases in heaviest precipitation events (above the 95th percentile) since 1950 in Europe and North America.
Air holds 6–7.5% more water vapor per degree Celsius of warming in the lower atmosphere.
Floods and droughts are linked to outbreaks of waterborne infectious diseases.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · PRECIPITATION | HURRICANES | SEA LEVEL RISE

items.title: Climate Change Effects on Hurricanes and Sea Level Rise
items.content: Warmer ocean temperatures, due to greenhouse gas emissions, provide more energy for hurricanes, potentially increasing their intensity. Additionally, global sea levels have risen significantly, with projections indicating further increases by 2100.
keyPoints: 90% of excess heat since the 1960s absorbed by oceans, fueling hurricane activity.
Future tropical cyclones may see a 2–11% increase in intensity and 20% more rainfall per storm by 2100.
Global sea level rose ~200 mm from 1901 to 2010, with an accelerated rate of 3.2 mm annually from 1993 to 2010.
Projected global sea level rise by 2100: 0.8–2 m, with an annual increase of 8–16 mm at century's end.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · PRECIPITATION | HURRICANES | SEA LEVEL RISE

items.subtopic: EL NIÑO SOUTHERN OSCILLATION | POPULATION MIGRATION AND CONFLICT | EFFECTS OF CLIMATE CHANGE ON INFECTIOUS DISEASE
items.title: Impact of El Niño on Global Weather and Disease Outbreaks
items.content: El Niño Southern Oscillation (ENSO) leads to significant changes in global weather patterns, increasing the frequency and severity of extreme events. These events can result in higher morbidity and mortality rates due to associated natural disasters.
keyPoints: ENSO cycles occur every 4–5 years with El Niño characterized by warmer-than-average water temperatures.
El Niño is linked to extreme weather events, such as Hurricane Mitch, which had winds of 290 km/h and caused over 11,000 deaths.
Post-storm outbreaks of diseases like cholera, leptospirosis, and dengue are associated with El Niño events.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · EL NIÑO SOUTHERN OSCILLATION | POPULATION MIGRATION AND CONFLICT | EFFECTS OF CLIMATE CHANGE ON INFECTIOUS DISEASE

items.subtopic: EL NIÑO SOUTHERN OSCILLATION | POPULATION MIGRATION AND CONFLICT | EFFECTS OF CLIMATE CHANGE ON INFECTIOUS DISEASE
items.title: Climate Change, Migration, and Infectious Disease Risk
items.content: Climate change leads to human displacement due to sea level rise and extreme weather, increasing the risk of infectious diseases. Warmer temperatures can expand disease vectors' habitats, leading to outbreaks.
keyPoints: 8 million inhabitants in low-lying South Pacific islands are vulnerable to climate-induced migration.
A 2011 East Africa drought contributed to a Somali famine with refugee mortality rates reaching 7.4/10,000.
Climate change facilitates the spread of diseases like avian malaria by enabling transmission at higher elevations due to warmer temperatures.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · EL NIÑO SOUTHERN OSCILLATION | POPULATION MIGRATION AND CONFLICT | EFFECTS OF CLIMATE CHANGE ON INFECTIOUS DISEASE

items.title: Temperature's Role in Malaria Transmission
items.content: Higher temperatures increase mosquito activity and shorten parasite cycles, potentially expanding malaria's geographic range. However, the optimal temperature for transmission is ~25°C, with a viable range between 16°C and 34°C.
keyPoints: Optimal temperature for malaria transmission: ~25°C
Transmission temperature range: 16°C to 34°C
Highland areas in East Africa and South America are vulnerable due to rising temperatures
Asynchrony between parasite development and mosquito gonotrophic cycle can decrease vectorial capacity
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · VECTOR-BORNE DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Impact of Precipitation on Malaria Incidence
items.content: Malaria incidence is strongly correlated with surface-water availability for mosquito breeding. Increased rainfall variance strengthens this association, but extreme rainfall can flush larvae from breeding sites.
keyPoints: Strong correlation between Anopheles abundance and surface-water pools
Increased rainfall variance linked to higher disease incidence
Extreme rainfall may reduce mosquito larvae by flushing breeding sites
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · VECTOR-BORNE DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Temperature Influence on Dengue Transmission
items.content: Higher temperatures accelerate larval development and adult emergence of Aedes mosquitoes, enhancing dengue transmission. Optimal conditions occur around 32°C.
keyPoints: Viral replication in Rhesus model: <7 days at >32–35°C, ≥12 days at 30°C
Replication unlikely below 26°C
Peak dengue transmission temperature: ~32°C
Smaller daily temperature range increases transmission potential
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · VECTOR-BORNE DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Impact of Climate Change on Dengue and Other Arboviral Diseases
items.content: Climate change influences the distribution and incidence of vector-borne diseases, such as dengue, Zika virus, chikungunya, West Nile virus, and Lyme disease. The spread of these diseases is affected by climatic conditions that favor mosquito and tick vectors.
keyPoints: Dengue epidemics are linked to precipitation but also influenced by domestic water storage due to piped water supply.
Rainfall predicts the seasonal timing of dengue epidemics.
Aedes mosquitoes' distribution overlaps with current global dengue spread, but other factors affect disease incidence.
Climatic-epidemiologic modeling suggests significant shifts in vectorial capacity for dengue by century's end without mitigation of greenhouse gas emissions.
Zika virus spread to the Western Hemisphere was facilitated by air travel and optimal climate conditions during the 2015 El Niño event.
Chikungunya virus could establish itself in Western Europe if vectors are present, with suitable conditions predicted for the first half of the twenty-first century.
West Nile virus outbreak areas in North America may shift northward to southern Canada by century's end, depending on reservoir availability and mosquito control.
Lyme disease cases have increased geographically in North America and Europe, with earlier annual peaks (6 weeks earlier in 2019 compared to 25 years prior).
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · VECTOR-BORNE DISEASE (part 2)

items.title: Geographic Shifts of Vector-Borne Diseases Due to Climate Change
items.content: Climate change is expected to alter the geographic distribution of vector-borne diseases, with northward shifts in areas suitable for West Nile virus and Lyme disease due to warming temperatures.
keyPoints: West Nile virus favorable outbreak areas may shift northward from current hotspots like California Central Valley and southern Texas to upper Midwest, New England, and potentially southern Canada by century's end.
Ixodes scapularis tick vector for Lyme disease has expanded into Canada due to warming temperatures.
Lyme disease is the most commonly reported vector-borne disease in North America with ~60,000 cases annually.
The timing of Lyme disease peaks has shifted, arriving 6 weeks earlier than 25 years ago.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · VECTOR-BORNE DISEASE (part 2)

items.title: Impact of Heavy Rainfall on Waterborne Disease Outbreaks
items.content: Heavy rainfall events are a significant precursor to waterborne disease outbreaks, with 51% of such outbreaks in the U.S. preceded by precipitation levels above the 90th percentile.
keyPoints: 51% of waterborne disease outbreaks were preceded by high precipitation levels.
Most regions except the Southwest and Hawaii have seen increased heavy downpours.
Outbreaks from surface-water contamination occur within a month, while groundwater-related outbreaks take ≥2 months.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · WATERBORNE DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Rising Temperatures and Vibrio Species
items.content: Warmer temperatures are linked to increased proliferation of Vibrio species, leading to more frequent disease outbreaks.
keyPoints: Vibriosis cases in the U.S. roughly tripled from 1996–2010.
Highest vibriosis cases occur each summer.
Regions like the Baltic Sea experience faster warming and lower salt content, increasing Vibrio infection risk.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · WATERBORNE DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: El Niño's Influence on Infectious Disease Outbreaks
items.content: El Niño events are associated with increased outbreaks of diseases such as Rift Valley fever, cholera, and dengue due to climate changes.
keyPoints: El Niño conditions have been linked to Rift Valley fever outbreaks in Africa since the 1950s.
In South America, a $1^{	ext{o}}$C temperature rise correlates with a 20% increase in malaria incidence.
15–22% of dengue variance in Thailand from 1996-2005 is attributable to El Niño.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · WATERBORNE DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: WATERBORNE DISEASE (part 2) | CLIMATE CHANGE, POPULATION DISPLACEMENT, AND INFECTIOUS DISEASE EPIDEMICS
items.title: Impact of Climate Change on Vector-Borne Disease Emergence
items.content: Climate change, particularly through phenomena like El Niño, can significantly influence the emergence and spread of vector-borne diseases. The 2015-2016 ENSO event led to exceptionally high temperatures in South and Central America, increasing the vectorial capacity of Aedes aegypti mosquitoes, which are vectors for both Zika virus and dengue fever. Laboratory studies indicate that the temperature optimum for Zika is 5°C higher than for dengue, suggesting that extreme temperatures may have contributed to the extensive Zika epidemic.
keyPoints: El Niño event in winter of 2015–2016 linked to Zika emergence
Vectorial capacity of Ae. aegypti highest in 60 years prior to Zika epidemic
Temperature optimum for Zika virus is 5°C higher than dengue
Extreme temperatures may have contributed to the extent of the Zika epidemic
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · WATERBORNE DISEASE (part 2) | CLIMATE CHANGE, POPULATION DISPLACEMENT, AND INFECTIOUS DISEASE EPIDEMICS

items.subtopic: CLIMATE CHANGE, POPULATION DISPLACEMENT, AND INFECTIOUS DISEASE EPIDEMICS
items.title: Consequences of Climate-Driven Population Displacement on Infectious Disease Outbreaks
items.content: Climate change-induced events such as extreme precipitation can lead to large-scale population displacements, increasing the risk of infectious disease outbreaks. The 2022 flooding in Pakistan, caused by over three times the average rainfall, affected approximately 33 million people and resulted in significant displacement. This event created conditions conducive to vector breeding and waterborne diseases like diarrhea, cholera, and malaria. Climate attribution analysis indicates that such intense rainfall events have become more extreme due to warming temperatures.
keyPoints: August 2022 Pakistan flooding affected 33 million people
Over 1400 deaths and 1.7 million homes destroyed in Pakistan floods
Flooding led to outbreaks of diarrhea, cholera, and malaria
Climate attribution analysis links intense rainfall events to warming temperatures
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · WATERBORNE DISEASE (part 2) | CLIMATE CHANGE, POPULATION DISPLACEMENT, AND INFECTIOUS DISEASE EPIDEMICS

items.subtopic: A BROADER VIEW OF CLIMATE CHANGE AND HEALTH
items.title: Climate Change: Mitigation Strategies and Health Co-Benefits
items.content: Mitigation of climate change involves reducing greenhouse gas emissions, which has been agreed upon to limit global warming to <2°C above preindustrial levels by 2050. This requires a reduction in emissions by 40–70% below 2010 levels. Mitigation efforts not only address climate change but also offer health co-benefits such as improved air quality and reduced respiratory infections.
keyPoints: Global warming target: <2°C above preindustrial levels by 2050
Required reduction in greenhouse gas emissions: 40–70% below 2010 levels
Health co-benefits of mitigation include better air quality and lower incidence/severity of respiratory infections
Eliminating air quality pollutants from energy generation could avoid >53,000 premature deaths annually in the US
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · A BROADER VIEW OF CLIMATE CHANGE AND HEALTH

items.subtopic: A BROADER VIEW OF CLIMATE CHANGE AND HEALTH
items.title: Climate Change: Adaptation Strategies and Challenges
items.content: Adaptation to climate change involves secondary prevention measures aimed at reducing harms from extreme weather events like sea level rise, heat waves, floods, droughts, and wildfires. The effectiveness of adaptation is limited by the difficulty in predicting the precise impacts of these events.
keyPoints: Adaptation seeks to reduce harms from extreme weather events
Challenges include predicting location, duration, and severity of events
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · A BROADER VIEW OF CLIMATE CHANGE AND HEALTH

It occurs at a slower pace than previous changes.
Its primary cause is human activities, and it happens faster.
It only affects temperature, not other weather components.
items.explanation: The passage states that current climate change differs because its primary cause is human activities and its pace is faster than past changes.

items.question: Which greenhouse gas has had the greatest effect on climate since the Industrial Revolution?
items.explanation: Carbon dioxide, released from fossil fuel combustion and deforestation, has had the greatest effect on climate since the Industrial Revolution.

items.question: What is the primary effect of greenhouse gases on Earth's atmosphere?
options: They cool the planet by reflecting solar radiation.
They absorb infrared radiation and warm the planet.
They increase oxygen levels in the atmosphere.
items.explanation: Greenhouse gases absorb infrared radiation, acting as a blanket to keep more of the sun's solar radiation in the atmosphere.

items.subtopic: EL NIÑO SOUTHERN OSCILLATION | POPULATION MIGRATION AND CONFLICT | EFFECTS OF CLIMATE CHANGE ON INFECTIOUS DISEASE
items.question: What are the typical periodic changes associated with El Niño Southern Oscillation (ENSO)?
options: Periodic changes in water temperature in the eastern Pacific Ocean occurring roughly every 4–5 years.
Changes in atmospheric pressure over the Atlantic Ocean every 2-3 years.
Cyclical variations in sea level along the Indian Ocean coastlines.
Fluctuations in global wind patterns every decade.
items.explanation: ENSO refers to periodic changes in water temperature in the eastern Pacific Ocean occurring roughly every 4–5 years.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · EL NIÑO SOUTHERN OSCILLATION | POPULATION MIGRATION AND CONFLICT | EFFECTS OF CLIMATE CHANGE ON INFECTIOUS DISEASE

items.subtopic: EL NIÑO SOUTHERN OSCILLATION | POPULATION MIGRATION AND CONFLICT | EFFECTS OF CLIMATE CHANGE ON INFECTIOUS DISEASE

options: HIV/AIDS, hepatitis B, and typhoid fever.
Zika virus, Ebola, and yellow fever.
items.explanation: Outbreaks of cholera, leptospirosis, and dengue occurred in the aftermath of Hurricane Mitch.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · EL NIÑO SOUTHERN OSCILLATION | POPULATION MIGRATION AND CONFLICT | EFFECTS OF CLIMATE CHANGE ON INFECTIOUS DISEASE

items.question: What effect does a smaller daily temperature range have on dengue virus transmission?
items.explanation: The passage notes that a smaller daily temperature range corresponds to higher dengue virus transmission potential.

items.question: Which region is projected to become less suitable for malaria transmission by 2100 due to climate change?
items.explanation: The passage suggests that West African nations may become less suitable for malaria transmission by 2100 due to higher temperatures and drier climate.

items.question: Which factor is less consistently associated with dengue epidemics according to the literature?
items.explanation: The association between dengue epidemics and precipitation is less consistent due to the mosquito vector's reliance on domestic breeding sites.

items.question: What event in 2015 contributed to the optimal climate for Zika virus spread in the Americas?

items.question: Which region is expected to become more climatically suited to West Nile virus outbreaks by mid-century?

items.question: Which pathogens are most commonly involved in waterborne disease outbreaks following heavy precipitation?

items.question: Which factor is suggested to have contributed to the emergence of Zika virus during the winter of 2015–2016?
options: A strong ENSO event leading to exceptionally high temperatures in South and Central America.
Increased rainfall causing flooding in urban areas.
Introduction of a new mosquito species into the region.
Decreased vectorial capacity of Ae. aegypti.
items.explanation: El Niño contributed to high temperatures, enhancing the vectorial capacity of Ae. aegypti and facilitating Zika virus emergence.

It caused extensive flooding and displacement affecting millions.
It resulted in an immediate improvement in water quality.

items.question: What role does climate change play in population displacement and infectious diseases?
options: It reduces the risk of epidemic outbreaks.
It has no significant impact on migration patterns.
It pressures populations to move due to environmental stresses, increasing disease risks.
It ensures better detection and response to disease outbreaks.
items.explanation: Climate change causes environmental stresses leading to displacement, which increases the risk of infectious diseases.

items.subtopic: A BROADER VIEW OF CLIMATE CHANGE AND HEALTH
items.question: Which of the following is a primary prevention measure in response to climate change?
options: Reducing greenhouse gas emissions into the atmosphere.
Improving air quality through dietary changes.
Adapting infrastructure to withstand extreme weather events.
Increasing food security by enhancing agricultural practices.
items.explanation: Mitigation, which includes reducing greenhouse gas emissions, represents primary prevention in response to climate change.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · A BROADER VIEW OF CLIMATE CHANGE AND HEALTH

items.subtopic: A BROADER VIEW OF CLIMATE CHANGE AND HEALTH
items.question: What was a key outcome of the COP21 agreement held in Paris?
options: Establishing a global carbon market and nationally determined contributions.
Setting a warming target of <1.5°C above preindustrial levels by 2050.
Mandating immediate cessation of all fossil fuel use globally.
Creating international policies for universal healthcare access.
items.explanation: At COP21, a framework was established for a global carbon market and nationally determined contributions (NDCs) to meet the Paris Climate Agreement.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · A BROADER VIEW OF CLIMATE CHANGE AND HEALTH

items.subtopic: A BROADER VIEW OF CLIMATE CHANGE AND HEALTH
items.question: Which health benefit is associated with mitigation efforts that reduce air pollution?
options: Decreased incidence and severity of respiratory infections.
Increased cases of diabetes due to dietary changes.
Higher rates of cardiovascular disease from fossil fuel combustion.
Reduced water security leading to more infectious diseases.
items.explanation: Mitigation efforts that reduce air pollution confer health co-benefits, including lower incidence and severity of respiratory infections.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · A BROADER VIEW OF CLIMATE CHANGE AND HEALTH

items.statement: Climate change is the sole determinant of infectious disease epidemiology.
items.explanation: For any given infection, climate change is but one of many factors determining disease epidemiology.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · EL NIÑO SOUTHERN OSCILLATION | POPULATION MIGRATION AND CONFLICT | EFFECTS OF CLIMATE CHANGE ON INFECTIOUS DISEASE

items.subtopic: EL NIÑO SOUTHERN OSCILLATION | POPULATION MIGRATION AND CONFLICT | EFFECTS OF CLIMATE CHANGE ON INFECTIOUS DISEASE
items.statement: Warmer ocean temperatures can lead to faster growth of certain algae, resulting in disease outbreaks.
items.explanation: Warmer ocean temperatures cause Pseudonitzschia spp., blue-green cyanobacteria, and dinoflagellates to grow faster, leading to concurrent disease outbreaks.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · EL NIÑO SOUTHERN OSCILLATION | POPULATION MIGRATION AND CONFLICT | EFFECTS OF CLIMATE CHANGE ON INFECTIOUS DISEASE

items.statement: Rising temperatures always increase the incidence of malaria.
items.explanation: The passage states that malaria incidence is not linearly associated with temperature and can decrease due to asynchrony in temperature-sensitive processes.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · VECTOR-BORNE DISEASE (part 1)

items.statement: Climate models account for the capacity of malaria control programs.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that climate models do not account for the capacity of malaria control programs to halt disease spread.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · VECTOR-BORNE DISEASE (part 1)

items.statement: The presence of Aedes mosquitoes alone is sufficient for dengue endemicity.
items.explanation: Other variables besides climate, such as domestic water storage and vector control, affect dengue incidence.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · VECTOR-BORNE DISEASE (part 2)

items.statement: The range of Ixodes scapularis has expanded into Canada due to warming temperatures.
items.explanation: Warming temperatures have allowed the primary tick vector for Lyme disease, Ixodes scapularis, to establish in Canada.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · VECTOR-BORNE DISEASE (part 2)

items.statement: Most regions of the United States, including the Southwest and Hawaii, have experienced an increase in heavy downpours.
items.explanation: The Southwest and Hawaii are exceptions to the general increase in heavy downpours in most U.S. regions.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · WATERBORNE DISEASE (part 1)

items.statement: El Niño events have been consistently associated with increased malaria incidence across all African countries.
items.explanation: The association between El Niño and malaria incidence in African countries has been inconsistent.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · WATERBORNE DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: WATERBORNE DISEASE (part 2) | CLIMATE CHANGE, POPULATION DISPLACEMENT, AND INFECTIOUS DISEASE EPIDEMICS
items.statement: Laboratory studies indicate that the temperature optimum for Zika virus is lower than for dengue.
items.explanation: The temperature optimum for Zika virus is 5°C warmer than for dengue.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · WATERBORNE DISEASE (part 2) | CLIMATE CHANGE, POPULATION DISPLACEMENT, AND INFECTIOUS DISEASE EPIDEMICS

items.subtopic: WATERBORNE DISEASE (part 2) | CLIMATE CHANGE, POPULATION DISPLACEMENT, AND INFECTIOUS DISEASE EPIDEMICS
items.statement: Climate attribution analysis suggests that intense rainfall over short periods has become more extreme with warming temperatures.
items.explanation: Analysis indicates increased extremity of short-period intense rainfall due to warming temperatures.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · WATERBORNE DISEASE (part 2) | CLIMATE CHANGE, POPULATION DISPLACEMENT, AND INFECTIOUS DISEASE EPIDEMICS

items.subtopic: A BROADER VIEW OF CLIMATE CHANGE AND HEALTH
items.statement: Climate adaptation is a form of primary prevention.
items.explanation: Climate adaptation is considered secondary prevention, as it seeks to reduce harms from climate change impacts.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · A BROADER VIEW OF CLIMATE CHANGE AND HEALTH

items.subtopic: A BROADER VIEW OF CLIMATE CHANGE AND HEALTH
items.statement: Long-term exposure to air pollution may increase mortality risk from COVID-19 and influenza.
items.explanation: Evidence has shown that long-term air pollution exposure can contribute to increased mortality risk from COVID-19 and influenza.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · A BROADER VIEW OF CLIMATE CHANGE AND HEALTH

items.subtopic: Climate Change and Infectious Disease | OVERVIEW
items.assertion: Current climate change may alter the incidence and severity of infectious diseases.
items.reason: The new climate has already altered the epidemiology of infectious diseases, and more greenhouse gases will further affect infections.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why current climate change may alter disease incidence and severity.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · Climate Change and Infectious Disease | OVERVIEW

items.subtopic: EL NIÑO SOUTHERN OSCILLATION | POPULATION MIGRATION AND CONFLICT | EFFECTS OF CLIMATE CHANGE ON INFECTIOUS DISEASE
items.assertion: Climate change may increase the frequency and severity of El Niño events.
items.reason: Evidence is accruing that climate change affects periodic changes in water temperature in the eastern Pacific Ocean.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why climate change may increase the frequency and severity of El Niño events.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · EL NIÑO SOUTHERN OSCILLATION | POPULATION MIGRATION AND CONFLICT | EFFECTS OF CLIMATE CHANGE ON INFECTIOUS DISEASE

items.assertion: Higher temperatures can increase the vectorial capacity of mosquitoes.
items.reason: Higher temperatures promote higher mosquito-biting rates and shorter parasite reproductive cycles.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why higher temperatures can increase vectorial capacity.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · VECTOR-BORNE DISEASE (part 1)

items.assertion: Extreme rainfall always increases malaria incidence.
items.reason: Extreme rainfall flushes mosquito larvae from breeding sites.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because extreme rainfall can counter disease-promoting effects by flushing larvae, while the reason is true.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · VECTOR-BORNE DISEASE (part 1)

items.assertion: Rising temperatures are associated with increased Vibrio species proliferation.
items.reason: Warmer temperatures favor the proliferation of Vibrio species, leading to more disease outbreaks.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · WATERBORNE DISEASE (part 1)

items.assertion: El Niño events have no impact on cholera epidemics in Bangladesh.
items.reason: During El Niño, warmer sea-surface temperatures and nutrient runoff lead to phytoplankton blooms that can amplify V. cholerae presence.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; there is a strong association between El Niño events and cholera epidemics in Bangladesh.
items.reference: Climate Change and Infectious Disease · WATERBORNE DISEASE (part 1)
